text: The surgeon introduces a Veress needle into the inferior crease of the umbilicus on a patient with a four-centimeter dermoid cyst on the left side. The needle insertion is confirmed by the three-click sound, followed by irrigation, suction, and the hanging drop test to ensure proper entry into the abdominal cavity. Pneumoperitoneum is then initiated.
The surgeon uses a preset pressure of fifteen millimeters of mercury to inflate the abdominal cavity with approximately 1.5 liters of gas, creating pneumoperitoneum for the cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon enlarges the incision to 11 mm and introduces an 11 mm optical trocar into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon dilates the inferior crease of the umbilicus and introduces an 11 mm optical trocar into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon connects the tubing and increases the pressure before introducing the telescope. The visual field reveals the sigmoid colon, uterus, and a dermoid cyst on the left side. An endo bag is introduced through the contralateral port, which is self-retaining.
A disposable endo bag made of high-quality polypropylene is introduced for use in the dermoid ovary cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully drops the cyst and ovary into the endo bag to prevent any potential spillage during the cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon observes the sigmoid colon, which appears adhered to the left side of the triangle of dome.
The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon, which is visually obstructing the surgical field, to prevent accidental contact with energy devices during the procedure.

The surgeon performs an ovarian cystectomy using a harmonic scalpel to open the outer ovarian cortex. A Maryland dissector and an atraumatic grasper are used to gently separate the cyst from surrounding tissue, avoiding direct grasping of the cyst with the Maryland instrument.

The surgeon carefully separates the ovarian cyst, ensuring not to hold the fimbria of the fallopian tube to avoid creating a stricture. The Maryland dissector is used with its convexity facing the cyst, employing a gentle pushing and pulling technique to prevent puncturing the cyst.
The surgeon removes the Maryland dissector and introduces an atraumatic grasper into the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector, noting that its pointed tip can potentially rupture the ovarian cyst if not handled carefully.
The surgeon identifies a clear dissection plane and uses the left hand to hold the ovarian cortex while the right hand carefully separates the cyst, placing it inside the endobag.
The surgeon applies gentle traction on the left side, gradually extracting the cyst from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to evenly manipulate and separate the cyst from surrounding tissues, ensuring balanced traction around the entire structure.
The surgeon slowly strips the cyst from surrounding tissue, ensuring careful manipulation to prevent rupture.
The surgeon gently maneuvers the cyst into the endobag, taking care to avoid spillage during the separation process.
The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to carefully separate the cyst from surrounding tissues, minimizing the risk of puncture.
The surgeon carefully encircles the cyst, ensuring equal tension is applied around its circumference to prevent spillage and maintain atraumatic handling.
The surgeon carefully attempts to remove the cyst intact using atraumatic techniques to avoid puncturing and spillage.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the cyst, with only 25% of it still attached, indicating a risk of puncture during the separation process.
The cyst is nearly extracted from the surrounding tissue, with no visible blood loss or bleeding observed during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully separates the last part of the cyst from the ovarian cortex, maintaining a safe distance from the fimbria to avoid damage.
The surgeon performs suction irrigation inside the cystic area after separation, ensuring the cavity is clear and checking for any signs of bleeding.
The surgical field shows no visible bleeding after the cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to desiccate small bleeding points on the ovarian cortex. The outer wall of the ovarian cortex is inverted, and the cyst is dropped into the ovarian fossa.

The surgeon carefully punctures the cyst inside the endo bag using a harmonic device to prevent spillage. The ovum forceps are used to drag the cystic content out once the mouth of the endo bag is exposed.

The surgeon extracts fatty content and hair balls from the dermoid cyst using ovum forceps. The cyst is punctured, and the contents are carefully pulled out. The endovac port is closed to prevent hernia.
